aliases: Pizza with Prawns Squid Mussels and Clams

english: Pizza with Prawns Squid Mussels and Clams

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pizza class (FOODON_00002475). No specific 'frutti di mare pizza' subclass exists; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pizza — seafood pizza (FoodEx2 broad category for pizza with seafood toppings)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated frutti di mare pizza code. A00LC (pizza) with seafood facet descriptor is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for frutti di mare pizza identified in the clinical terminology browser.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a whole 10–12 inch pizza weighing approximately 400 g, typically shared between two people or consumed as a single meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pizza with seafood, regular crust (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for 'frutti di mare pizza' exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for seafood pizza and pizza with mixed toppings (FDC IDs in the pizza and mixed-dish categories), cross-referenced with the Singapore HPB Foodo database for comparable mixed seafood pizza dishes. Input values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with USDA reference ranges and adjusted where discrepancies were identified. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA fatty acid profiles for comparable seafood pizza; water_g estimated from USDA moisture data for pizza with seafood toppings.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for frutti di mare pizza exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'pizza, cheese' (GI ~60, white flour base, regular crust) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The seafood topping does not materially alter the GI relative to the dough base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (60) × carbohydrate per serving (95 g from 400 g × 23.75 g/100g) / 100 = 57.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg/100 g; a 400 g whole pizza delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit intake to 1–2 slices, request reduced-cheese and lighter tomato sauce preparation, and avoid adding extra salt or processed seafood to keep cumulative daily sodium within the 2000 mg WHO target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white-flour pizza base with GI ~60 and carbohydrate 23.75 g/100 g; a whole pizza delivers ~95 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~57, causing significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to 1–2 slices, request a whole-wheat or thin crust to lower the glycemic index and increase dietary fibre, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.5 g/100 g and sodium 450 mg/100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; however, seafood toppings provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA from mussels, clams, and prawns).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 1–2 slices to control saturated fat and sodium intake, choose thin-crust preparation, and note that the seafood component provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that partially offset cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood toppings provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; however, tomato sauce contains nightshade alkaloids (solanine) that may aggravate RA symptoms in sensitive individuals, and sodium at 450 mg/100 g may worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of the seafood toppings but should monitor nightshade tolerance, request reduced-sodium preparation, and choose whole-wheat crust to lower the glycemic load that can drive inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Frutti di Mare Pizza (Italian: 'fruits of the sea pizza') is a classic Italian pizza featuring a generous assortment of Mediterranean seafood — typically prawns, squid, mussels, and clams — arranged over a tomato-sauce base and topped with mozzarella cheese, then baked in a high-temperature oven until the crust is golden and the cheese is melted and lightly browned. The pizza dough is made from refined white wheat flour, giving the crust a soft, chewy interior with a crisp outer edge. The seafood toppings contribute a briny, oceanic flavour balanced by the acidity of the tomato sauce and the mild creaminess of the mozzarella. Olive oil, garlic, fresh basil, and oregano are commonly used as finishing aromatics. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 238 kcal, 13.75 g protein, 10 g fat (4.5 g saturated), 23.75 g carbohydrate, 1.25 g dietary fibre, and 450 mg sodium. The seafood component contributes lean protein, omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA from mussels and clams), zinc, iron, and vitamin B12. Clinically, the primary concerns are the high sodium content (450 mg/100 g) relevant to hypertension management, and the moderate-to-high glycemic load from the refined flour base relevant to type 2 diabetes management. The saturated fat level (4.5 g/100 g) is moderate and relevant to cardiovascular disease risk. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish, crustacean, gluten, or dairy allergies. It is widely served in Italian restaurants and pizza chains across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo entry for frutti di mare pizza exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy seafood pizza composites and cross-referenced with the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). The macronutrient profile is internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database cheese pizza entry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy seafood pizza and mixed-dish pizza composites; no exact frutti di mare pizza entry exists in USDA or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024. Input ai_generated values were retained where consistent with USDA reference ranges. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.5) are estimated from USDA fatty acid profiles for comparable seafood pizza dishes; these are approximate. 3. water_g (52.0) is estimated from USDA moisture data for pizza with seafood toppings. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific composite seafood pizza preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024. 5. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on cheese pizza (white flour base) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for frutti di mare pizza exists. Glycemic load per serving = 60 × (400 × 0.2375) / 100 = 57.0. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; mozzarella and seafood sourcing would need to be verified for halal certification. 7. The dish is served in Singapore as an Italian restaurant and pizza-chain offering; country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore reflecting the serving context rather than the dish's Italian culinary origin.
